content.text: FastAPI provides one of the most complete and ergonomic systems for handling HTTP request data and shaping response output among all Python web frameworks — combining automatic parsing, validation, serialization, and documentation generation from a single source of truth.

Request and response handling in FastAPI refers to the full lifecycle of an HTTP interaction: extracting and validating incoming data (path parameters, query strings, headers, cookies, request bodies), and controlling the structure, format, status codes, and headers of outgoing responses.
FastAPI builds this system on top of
for the underlying HTTP primitives, exposing a declarative, type-annotation-driven API that eliminates most boilerplate while generating OpenAPI documentation automatically.

In any web API, the boundary between raw HTTP bytes and application logic is where most bugs, security issues, and maintenance problems originate. Frameworks that leave parsing and validation to developers produce inconsistent error handling, missing input checks, undocumented contracts, and brittle response shapes.
FastAPI's approach solves several problems simultaneously:
— invalid inputs are rejected before they reach business logic
— type annotations drive validation, serialization, and API docs simultaneously
— validation failures produce structured, predictable 422 responses automatically
— returning a Pydantic model or dict auto-serializes to JSON with correct content-type headers
FastAPI categorizes incoming HTTP data into distinct sources, each with its own declaration mechanism:
Path parameters are declared by naming a function argument to match the
in the route path. FastAPI automatically coerces and validates the type.
FastAPI documents the enum values in OpenAPI automatically and rejects any value outside the defined set.
Any function parameter not present in the route path is treated as a query parameter.
maps directly to the function arguments. Optional parameters with defaults don't need to be present in the URL.

from typing import Optional

@app.get("/items/")
async def search_items(
    q: Optional[str] = None,       # Optional — defaults to None
    active: bool = True,            # Optional with default
    category: str = ...,            # Required — no default
):
    ...
dependency. FastAPI automatically converts Python's underscore naming convention to HTTP's hyphen convention (
The request body is declared by typing a parameter as a Pydantic model. FastAPI parses the JSON body, validates it against the model, and injects the typed object.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional

class Item(BaseModel):
    name: str
    description: Optional[str] = None
    price: float = Field(..., gt=0, description="Price must be positive")
    tax: Optional[float] = None

@app.post("/items/")
async def create_item(item: Item):
    total = item.price + (item.tax or 0)
    return {"name": item.name, "total_price": total}
Pydantic validates types, constraints, and required fields. Invalid payloads return a detailed 422 response listing every field error.

from typing import List, Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Image(BaseModel):
    url: str
    alt_text: Optional[str] = None

class Product(BaseModel):
    name: str
    tags: List[str] = []
    images: List[Image] = []

@app.post("/products/")
async def create_product(product: Product):
    return product
Pydantic handles arbitrarily deep nesting with full validation at every level.
FastAPI correctly identifies each parameter's source from context — no annotations needed to distinguish them beyond their types.
cannot be mixed in the same endpoint — a request is either JSON or

from typing import List

@app.post("/uploads/")
async def upload_multiple(files: List[UploadFile] = File(...)):
    return [{"filename": f.filename, "size": (await f.read()).__len__()} for f in files]
object is available as a parameter for low-level access:

from fastapi import Request

@app.get("/raw/")
async def raw_request(request: Request):
    return {
        "method": request.method,
        "url": str(request.url),
        "client_ip": request.client.host,
        "headers": dict(request.headers),
        "query_params": dict(request.query_params),
        "path_params": request.path_params,
    }
For reading the raw body directly:
, or Pydantic model from any endpoint auto-serializes to JSON with

from fastapi import status

@app.post("/items/", status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED)
async def create_item(item: Item):
    return item

@app.delete("/items/{item_id}", status_code=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT)
async def delete_item(item_id: int):
    return None  # No body for 204
parameter controls what gets serialized back to the client — regardless of what the function actually returns. This is a critical security and API contract feature.

class UserIn(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password: str      # Accepted on input
    email: str

class UserOut(BaseModel):
    username: str
    email: str         # Password NOT included in output

@app.post("/users/", response_model=UserOut)
async def create_user(user: UserIn):
    # Even if we accidentally return the full user object,
    # FastAPI will only serialize fields defined in UserOut
    return user

@app.get(
    "/items/{item_id}",
    response_model=Item,
    response_model_exclude_unset=True,    # Omit fields not set (not just default)
    response_model_exclude_none=True,     # Omit None fields
    response_model_include={"name", "price"},  # Whitelist fields
    response_model_exclude={"internal_id"},    # Blacklist fields
)
async def get_item(item_id: int):
    ...
This is especially useful for PATCH semantics — returning only fields that were explicitly set, not all defaults:

@app.patch("/items/{item_id}", response_model=Item, response_model_exclude_unset=True)
async def partial_update(item_id: int, item: Item):
    # Only fields explicitly provided in the response appear in output
    return item
FastAPI provides specialized response classes for different content types:

from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse @app.get("/page/", response_class=HTMLResponse) async def serve_html(): return "

Hello

"

from fastapi.responses import PlainTextResponse

@app.get("/text/", response_class=PlainTextResponse)
async def plain():
    return "Hello, plain text"
For large files or real-time data that should not be buffered in memory:
For serving complete files from disk efficiently:
is significantly faster than Python's built-in

@app.post("/session/")
async def create_session(response: Response):
    response.set_cookie(
        key="session_id",
        value="abc123",
        httponly=True,
        secure=True,
        samesite="lax",
        max_age=3600
    )
    return {"message": "Session created"}
By default, validation errors return a
with Pydantic's error format. To customize:

HTTP Request
     │
     ▼
Uvicorn (ASGI) ──► FastAPI App
     │
     ▼
Middleware Stack (auth, logging, CORS, etc.)
     │
     ▼
Router — matches path + method
     │
     ▼
Dependency Injection resolution
     │
     ▼
Parameter Extraction & Validation
  ├── Path params   → coerced + validated
  ├── Query params  → coerced + validated
  ├── Headers       → extracted
  ├── Cookies       → extracted
  └── Body          → parsed + Pydantic validated
          │
          ▼ (422 if any validation fails)
     │
     ▼
Endpoint Function executes
     │
     ▼
Return value → Response Model filtering
     │
     ▼
Serialization (JSON / HTML / bytes / stream)
     │
     ▼
Response headers, cookies set
     │
     ▼
HTTP Response sent to client
demonstrates the full request/response system working together:

from fastapi import FastAPI, Header, Depends, HTTPException, status, Response
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator
from typing import List, Optional
from enum import Enum
import uuid

app = FastAPI()

class Currency(str, Enum):
    USD = "USD"
    EUR = "EUR"
    GBP = "GBP"

class OrderItem(BaseModel):
    product_id: str = Field(..., min_length=3, max_length=50)
    quantity: int = Field(..., ge=1, le=100)
    unit_price: float = Field(..., gt=0)

class ShippingAddress(BaseModel):
    street: str
    city: str
    country: str = Field(..., min_length=2, max_length=2)  # ISO country code
    postal_code: str

class CreateOrderRequest(BaseModel):
    items: List[OrderItem] = Field(..., min_length=1)
    shipping_address: ShippingAddress
    currency: Currency = Currency.USD
    coupon_code: Optional[str] = None

    @field_validator("items")
    @classmethod
    def validate_no_duplicate_products(cls, items):
        product_ids = [i.product_id for i in items]
        if len(product_ids) != len(set(product_ids)):
            raise ValueError("Duplicate product IDs are not allowed")
        return items

class OrderItemResponse(BaseModel):
    product_id: str
    quantity: int
    unit_price: float
    line_total: float

class CreateOrderResponse(BaseModel):
    order_id: str
    status: str
    items: List[OrderItemResponse]
    subtotal: float
    currency: Currency

@app.post(
    "/orders/",
    response_model=CreateOrderResponse,
    status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED,
    response_model_exclude_unset=True,
)
async def create_order(
    order: CreateOrderRequest,
    response: Response,
    idempotency_key: Optional[str] = Header(default=None),
    x_tenant_id: str = Header(...),  # Required tenant header
):
    # Validate tenant
    if not is_valid_tenant(x_tenant_id):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="Invalid tenant")

    # Idempotency check
    if idempotency_key:
        cached = get_cached_order(idempotency_key)
        if cached:
            response.status_code = status.HTTP_200_OK
            return cached

    # Build response
    order_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    items_response = [
        OrderItemResponse(
            product_id=item.product_id,
            quantity=item.quantity,
            unit_price=item.unit_price,
            line_total=item.quantity * item.unit_price,
        )
        for item in order.items
    ]

    result = CreateOrderResponse(
        order_id=order_id,
        status="pending",
        items=items_response,
        subtotal=sum(i.line_total for i in items_response),
        currency=order.currency,
    )

    # Set response headers
    response.headers["X-Order-ID"] = order_id
    response.headers["Location"] = f"/orders/{order_id}"

    return result

This example demonstrates: nested Pydantic validation with custom validators, required and optional headers, response model filtering, dynamic status code changes, custom response headers, and enum-constrained fields — all in a single, self-documenting endpoint.
bypasses validation and loses type safety. Always prefer typed Pydantic model parameters.
, whatever the function returns is serialized in full. A function returning a DB model object with a
field will expose it. Always define explicit output models.

content = await file.read()   # Reads entire file
size = len(content)           # OK
content_again = await file.read()  # Returns empty bytes — already at EOF
before re-reading, or read once and reuse the variable.
FastAPI cannot parse both JSON body and form data in the same request. Use one or the other.

# BAD: trusting client-supplied content_type
if file.content_type == "image/jpeg":
    process(file)

# BETTER: validate actual file signature (magic bytes)
content = await file.read()
if not content.startswith(b'\xff\xd8\xff'):  # JPEG magic bytes
    raise HTTPException(400, "Not a valid JPEG")
fields clutters the response and leaks schema information. Use

# This does NOT work — status_code in decorator is fixed at decoration time
@app.post("/items/", status_code=201)
async def create(item: Item):
    return item  # Can't change status code from here without Response parameter
To conditionally change the status code, inject the
for production endpoints — it enforces output contracts and prevents accidental data leakage.
for field-level validation constraints and descriptions — these appear in OpenAPI docs.
) instead of raw integers for readability (
for file uploads — it avoids loading the entire file into memory upfront.
for files larger than a few MB to avoid memory exhaustion.
for high-throughput endpoints with large JSON payloads.
classes — they produce better error messages and OpenAPI schema than free-string validation.
declarations — they enrich auto-generated docs with no runtime cost.
for cross-field or complex validation logic rather than adding it to endpoint functions.
on all authentication cookies to prevent XSS and MITM exposure.
FastAPI 0.100+ defaults to Pydantic v2, which is implemented in Rust via
compared to Pydantic v1 for typical request models. For APIs with heavy validation, this is a significant free win when upgrading.
For large response payloads (thousands of items, deep nesting), Python's built-in
module can become a CPU bottleneck. Switching to
Loading a 500MB file into memory to serve it as a download can exhaust container memory under concurrent load. Always stream large files:
Pydantic validation runs on every request. For extremely high-throughput endpoints where the payload is already trusted (e.g., internal service-to-service calls), consider using
(which skips validation) on the response side rather than the request side.
Input Validation as a Security Layer
FastAPI's automatic validation prevents entire classes of attack vectors:
— an integer field that receives a string or array is rejected before reaching business logic.
on string fields limits memory consumption from malicious payloads.
— by default, Pydantic v2 ignores extra fields; set
to reject requests with undeclared fields entirely.

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

class StrictItem(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    name: str
    price: float
Always validate file size before reading fully into memory — check
header or read in bounded chunks.
Validate actual file content (magic bytes), not just
Store uploaded files outside the web root and serve through controlled endpoints, not directly.
FastAPI maps HTTP request data (path, query, headers, cookies, body) to function parameters using type annotations, with automatic coercion and validation requiring zero manual parsing code.
parameter is not optional in production — it enforces output contracts and prevents accidental serialization of sensitive internal fields.
all accept validation constraints that are automatically enforced at runtime and documented in OpenAPI.
for file handling — it avoids full memory loading and provides metadata like filename and content type.
is essential for large file downloads or real-time data generation; buffering large responses in memory creates memory exhaustion risk under concurrent load.
should be the default response class for high-throughput, large-payload endpoints — it offers significantly faster serialization than the standard
FastAPI's validation errors (422 responses) are structured and detailed by default; they can be overridden with a custom exception handler to match API error format conventions.
File uploads must be validated both for content type (via magic bytes, not MIME headers) and for size to prevent memory exhaustion and polyglot file attacks.
